elements.sent-2411-001: Quantum models of interacting bosons have wide range of applications, among them the propagation of optical modes in nonlinear media, such as the $k$-photon down conversion.
elements.sent-2411-002: Many of such models are related to nonlinear deformations of finite group algebras, thus, in this sense, they are exactly solvable.
elements.sent-2411-003: Whereas the advanced group-theoretic methods have been developed to study the eigenvalue spectrum of exactly solvable Hamiltonians, in quantum optics the prime interest is not the spectrum of the Hamiltonian, but the evolution of an initial state, such as the generation of optical signal modes by a strong pump mode propagating in a nonlinear medium.
elements.sent-2411-004: I propose a simple and general method of derivation of the solution to such a state evolution problem, applicable to a wide class of quantum models of interacting bosons.
elements.sent-2411-005: For the $k$-photon down conversion model and its generalizations, the solution to the state evolution problem is given in the form of an infinite series expansion in the powers of propagation time with the coefficients defined by a recursion relation with a single polynomial function, unique for each nonlinear model.
elements.sent-2411-006: As an application, I compare the exact solution to the parametric down conversion process with the semiclassical parametric approximation.
elements.sent-2411-007: Finding the exact solution to a physically relevant model is of high value even in the age of widespread computer simulations.
elements.sent-2411-008: Some crucial features of the solution, such as the asymptotic or qualitative behavior, could be only studied by the analytical approach.
elements.sent-2411-009: Widely applicable algebraic methods have been discovered in order to derive exactly solvable models and to obtain the exact solutions to the eigenvalue problem.
elements.sent-2411-010: The first of such methods was the famous Bethe's ansatz for the Heisenberg antiferromagnetic chain model \cite{BetheAnsatz , extended also to the Bose gas with the zero-range interaction potential \cite{BoseGas and to the one-dimensional Hubbard model \cite{1DHubbard .}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Other algebraic methods have been developed in order to find and analyze exactly solvable models in Quantum Mechanics, e.g., the method of factorization of second-order differential operator (representing the quantum Hamiltonian) into a product of two first-order differential (ladder) operators \cite{Factoriz , the Darboux transformations \cite{Darboux , and the Quantum Inverse Scattering Method \cite{QISM .}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: Exactly solvable models are abundant also in quantum optics.
elements.sent-2411-013: The effective potential method for the generating function approach has been found for the Dicke model \cite{DickeModel and the spin systems \cite{SpinSystems , the Bethe ansatz allows to derive the energy spectra of the three-boson model \cite{3BosonBethe .}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Moreover, it was found that some quantum Hamiltonians of the forth order in the boson creation and annihilation operators allow exact solutions for a part of the energy spectra subject to a hidden symmetry \cite{4BosonModels .}
elements.sent-2411-015: The latter models, which include the $k$-photon down conversion and multiple photon cascades, are the so-called quasi-exactly solvable models, allowing for analytical analysis of some part of the energy spectra \cite{QESMod .}
elements.sent-2411-016: With the help of the group-theoretic methods the Hamiltonian can sometimes be mapped onto the generators of a deformed Lie algebra, which allows one to derive the equations for the eigenvalues and eigenfunctions of a nonlinear quantum optical Hamiltonian, including the second-harmonic generation model \cite{GroupMeth,LieAlg3Boson,GroupMeth2 .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Such methods have been applied recently to find the energy spectra for a wide range of such nonlinear boson models \cite{LieAlgGen1,LieAlgGen2 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: In the ever growing field of research on exactly solvable models, the focus is usually on finding the spectrum of the Hamiltonian and the associated eigenstates.
elements.sent-2411-019: On the other hand, in quantum optics applications it is more important to solve the initial value problem, whereas the eigenstates of the full Hamiltonian do not correspond to the optical energy (given by the free propagation part of the Hamiltonian, see below).
elements.sent-2411-020: The most important example is the work-horse of all quantum optics -- the two-photon parametric down conversion process in a nonlinear medium with the second-order nonlinearity \cite{Wu86,Slusher87 (see also the reviews \cite{ReviewSq,Couteau18,Zhang21 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-021: The recent experimental realization of the long sought three-photon spontaneous parametric down-conversion \cite{3phDC adds another integrable model to applications in quantum optics.}
elements.sent-2411-022: The analytical approaches developed previously for deriving the analytical results for the exactly solvable models, such as the deformed Lie algebras and the Quantum Inverse Scattering Method, are quite involved in their technical part, which may explain the fact that the same models in the physical literature, such as the $k$-photon down-conversion \cite{ClasskSq,GenSqueez , have been studied analytically by other methods, such as the semiclassical or the WKB-like approximations \cite{WKB4GenFun,Hillery84, WKBpump, Crouch88 , or resorting to numerical simulations \cite{Reid88, Drobny92, Buzek93, Drobny94, Hillery95 helped by the reductions based on the conservation laws.}}}

elements.sent-2411-024: The purpose of the present work is propose a simple algebraic method of derivation of exact solution to the state evolution problem for a wide class of integrable models of interacting bosons.
elements.sent-2411-025: The method is used to derive the exact solution to the problem of generation of optical signal modes by a pump mode propagating in a nonlinear medium for a wide class of quantum optical models, such as the $k$-photon down-conversion and the related models.

elements.section-2411-002: Models of interacting bosons in quantum optics
elements.sent-2411-031: In quantum optics the models of interacting bosons appear due to propagation of optical light modes in a nonlinear media, when the phase matching conditions are satisfied in the medium.
elements.sent-2411-032: One is usually interested in the generation of signal modes from the vacuum state by the strong pump mode(s).
elements.sent-2411-033: In a lossless medium, by the energy conservation (i.e., the Manley–Rowe relations, see for more details Ref. \cite{NonlOpt ) the total electromagnetic field energy is conserved during the propagation.}
elements.sent-2411-034: The resulting model Hamiltonian can be partitioned, accordingly, into two terms, the free propagation term, $\hat{H _0$, which corresponds to the total electromagnetic energy and is quadratic in the boson creation and annihilation operators of the optical modes, and the interaction term, $\hat{H _{1}$, of higher-order in the boson operators, which describes the photon conversion between the optical modes propagating in the nonlinear medium.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: When the phase matching conditions are satisfied, the interaction part of the Hamiltonian preserves the total optical energy: $[\hat{H _0,\hat{H _1]=0$.}}

elements.sent-2411-184: In the present work the exact solution was derived to a wide class of nonlinear models describing interacting bosons, with the immediate application to the state evolution problem as it is posed in the realm of quantum optics, where a pump mode propagating in a nonlinear optical media satisfying the phase matching conditions generates the signal mode(s) which are initially in the vacuum state.
elements.sent-2411-185: The solution to the state evolution problem is given as a series expansion in powers of the propagation time, where a single function, polynomial in the signal mode(s) populations, represents the specific quantum model.
elements.sent-2411-186: The results have immediate application to the nonlinear models in quantum optics, such as the $k$-photon down conversion process, important for the development of quantum technology.
elements.sent-2411-187: A number of open problems is left for the future work.
elements.sent-2411-188: How to generalize the method in order to solve the state evolution problem for an arbitrary initial state of the system? Another open problem is related to the semiclassical approach usually applied in quantum optics: What is the correct asymptotic limit of the exact solution when the dimension of the invariant subspace (e.g., the average number of photons in the pump mode) goes to infinity? The hope is that the present approach paved the way to solve such open problems.
elements.sent-2411-189: This work was partially supported by the National Council for Scientific and Technological Development (CNPq).
elements.sent-2411-190: Quantum models of interacting bosons have a wide range of applications, including the propagation of optical modes in nonlinear media, such as the $k$-photon down-conversion.
elements.sent-2411-191: Many of these models are related to nonlinear deformations of finite group algebras and, in this sense, are exactly solvable.
elements.sent-2411-192: While advanced group-theoretic methods were developed to study the eigenvalue spectrum, in quantum optics, the primary focus is not on the spectrum of the Hamiltonian but rather on the evolution of an initial state -- such as the generation of optical signal modes by a strong pump mode propagating through a nonlinear medium.
elements.sent-2411-193: I propose a simple and general method to solve the state evolution problem, applicable to a broad class of quantum models of interacting bosons.
elements.sent-2411-194: For the k-photon down-conversion model and its generalizations, the solution to the state evolution problem is expressed as an infinite series expansion in powers of the propagation time, with coefficients determined by a recursion relation involving only a single polynomial function.
elements.sent-2411-195: This polynomial function is unique to each nonlinear model.
elements.sent-2411-196: As an application, I compare the exact solution of the parametric down-conversion process with the semiclassical parametric approximation.
elements.sent-2411-197: Obtaining an exact solution to a physically relevant model remains highly valuable, even in the era of widespread computer simulations.
elements.sent-2411-198: Some crucial features of the solution, such as the asymptotic or qualitative behavior, can only be studied by the analytical approach.
elements.sent-2411-199: Over time, widely applicable algebraic methods have been developed to derive exactly solvable models and obtain exact solutions to eigenvalue problems.
elements.sent-2411-200: One of the earliest such methods was Bethe’s ansatz for the Heisenberg antiferromagnetic chain model \cite{BetheAnsatz , later extended to the Bose gas with a zero-range interaction potential \cite{BoseGas and the one-dimensional Hubbard model \cite{1DHubbard .}}}
elements.sent-2411-201: Other algebraic techniques have since been introduced, such as the factorization method for second-order differential operators, which expresses the quantum Hamiltonian as a product of two first-order ladder operators \cite{Factoriz , the Darboux transformations \cite{Darboux , and the Quantum Inverse Scattering Method \cite{QISM .}}}
elements.sent-2411-202: Exactly solvable models are also prevalent in quantum optics.
elements.sent-2411-203: The effective potential method for generating function approach has been applied to the Dicke model \cite{DickeModel and spin systems \cite{SpinSystems .}}
elements.sent-2411-204: Additionally, the Bethe ansatz has been used to derive the energy spectra of the three-boson model \cite{3BosonBethe .}

elements.sent-2411-411: In this work, we have derived the exact solution for a broad class of nonlinear models describing interacting bosons, with immediate applications to the state evolution problem in quantum optics.
elements.sent-2411-412: Specifically, we have considered a pump mode propagating through a nonlinear optical medium that satisfies the phase-matching conditions, thereby generating signal mode(s) initially in the vacuum state.
elements.sent-2411-413: The solution to the state evolution problem is expressed as a series expansion in powers of the propagation time, where a single function—polynomial in the signal mode(s) populations—characterizes the specific quantum model.
elements.sent-2411-414: These results have direct applications to nonlinear models in quantum optics, including the $k$-photon down-conversion process, which plays a crucial role in advancing quantum technology.
elements.sent-2411-415: Several open problems remain for future research.
elements.sent-2411-416: One key challenge is how to generalize the method to solve the state evolution problem for an arbitrary initial state.
elements.sent-2411-417: Another open question concerns the semiclassical approach commonly used in quantum optics: What is the correct asymptotic limit of the exact solution when the dimension of the invariant subspace (e.g., the average number of photons in the pump mode) approaches infinity? It is hoped that the present approach has laid the foundation for addressing such open problems.
